narrative: As a Black male student applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, an LSAT score of 24, and your position in the lower deciles for your Year 1 and Year 3 school grades—specifically the 3rd decile early on and 2nd decile by Year 3—which are below the typical student's standings. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, and you've indicated plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated an 82% chance that you would fail the bar exam, leading directly to the rejection of your application since the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass on their first try. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key factors from your profile that stood out the most. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your LSAT score, which is notably lower than the average of around 37 seen in the dataset, signaling to the model a potential gap in the standardized reasoning skills tied to bar success. Next in importance was your Year 3 decile ranking, even lower than the typical 5th or 6th decile, suggesting your academic performance trended downward over time compared to peers. Your undergraduate GPA actually worked slightly in your favor, pulling the prediction a bit toward passing since, while below the average 3.21, it wasn't as detrimental as the other top factors. Overall, these elements made your profile riskier than most applicants, where stronger test scores and consistent rankings align with higher bar passage rates. It's understandable to feel discouraged, especially given the systemic challenges many Black students like yourself face in competitive fields like law. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and potential. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift the model's view in your favor, opening doors to programs that value your unique background. Keep pushing—you've got the determination to rewrite this story.
